one_line_takeaway: Replacing KGW's two constant watermark hyperparameters with per-token MLP-predicted values, jointly trained by multi-objective optimization on a differentiable z-score and a SimCSE semantic loss, pushes the detectability-vs-coherence Pareto frontier past prior LLM watermarks (up to TPR 1.0 at 0% FPR vs EXP-edit's 0.922) at near-KGW cost.
key_finding: The learned per-token parameters improve the detectability-vs-semantic-coherence Pareto frontier over every baseline. Against EXP-edit (Table 1), Ours (Top-k=50) reaches TPR 1.000 @0% FPR and 1.000 @1% FPR with SimCSE 0.713, versus EXP-edit's 0.922/0.996/0.655 and EXP-edit(Top-k=50)'s 0.968/0.996/0.677. It beats KGW, SWEET/SWEET-NoPrompt, MultiBit, and SIR/SIR-NoPrompt on the Pareto curves for OPT-1.3B, and the OPT-trained generators still improve on KGW when transferred to LLAMA2 7B/13B/70B. Computationally it is near-free relative to KGW (generation 3.946s vs 3.827s for 200 tokens; detection 0.166s), and dramatically cheaper than EXP-edit (24.693s generation, 155.045s detection). It is also more robust than KGW under Dipper paraphrase and both copy-paste attacks. A POS analysis shows the model learns interpretable behavior: it lowers gamma/delta after adjectives and determiners (which usually precede a highly-constrained noun) and raises them after punctuation (where the next token is loosely constrained), which also explains paraphrase robustness since punctuation patterns survive rephrasing.
core_contribution: A token-specific inference-time watermarking method for LLMs that replaces KGW's two constant hyperparameters (the green-list splitting ratio gamma and the watermark logit delta) with two lightweight MLPs, a gamma-generator and a delta-generator, that emit per-token gamma_t and delta_t from the embedding of the preceding token. The two networks are trained under a multi-objective optimization (MOO) framework using the Multiple-Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) to jointly minimize a detection loss and a semantic loss, seeking a Pareto-optimal trade-off between watermark detectability and semantic coherence. Key technical enablers are (1) a differentiable relaxed z-score (relaxing the green-token count |s|_G into a sum of green-selection probabilities) so detectability can be optimized by gradient descent, (2) a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation of the Bernoulli green/red list assignment so the dynamic gamma is differentiable, and (3) Theorem 4.1 showing the green-token count under H0 is Poisson-binomial and Gaussian-approximable, yielding a corrected z-score for a non-constant gamma_t (Eq. 3). This is the paper's OWN contribution, distinct from the cited KGW/SWEET/SIR/EXP-edit baselines.
threat_model: The defender is a model owner / regulator who wants to reliably distinguish LLM-generated text from human-written text to counter misinformation, fake news, election-manipulation content, and academic dishonesty, and to protect IP. Detection is via a one-sided z-test over green-list tokens and (unlike SWEET/SIR) does not require the original prompt, only the LLM embedding matrix plus the hash/RNG. The adversary tries to strip the watermark while preserving meaning via (a) the Dipper paraphrase attack (T5-XXL-based, lex=40/div=100) and (b) copy-paste attacks (Copy-Paste-1 and Copy-Paste-3) that dilute watermarked spans inside human text. The paper's own Impact Statement flags the counter-risk: mislabeling genuine human text as machine-generated could cause false plagiarism accusations.
limitations: The generators are trained only on OPT-1.3B and C4 news text; cross-model results (LLAMA2 7B/13B/70B) and the robustness study compare only against KGW, not the full baseline set. Absolute semantic-similarity values are modest and vary by setup (roughly 0.67-0.74 SimCSE across figures), so gains are relative-Pareto improvements rather than high absolute fidelity. The method still shifts the output distribution via delta (unlike distortion-free EXP-edit), so it is not statistically indistinguishable. Detection needs the LLM embedding matrix (a mild extra requirement over vanilla KGW). Fixed FPR thresholds are estimated from a 500-text held-out human set. The authors' own Impact Statement concedes the false-positive/false-accusation risk against human authors.

datasets_used: C4 news-like subset (Raffel et al. 2019): 6,400 train / 500 validation / 500 test texts, filtered to 500-1000 tokens, last 200 tokens as human baseline completion
Dipper paraphrase model (T5-XXL 11B, lex=40 div=100) used to generate the paraphrase attack
quantitative_results: Generation time for 200 tokens on OPT-1.3B: Ours 3.946s, KGW 3.827s, No-Watermark 3.220s, SWEET 4.030s, SIR 8.420s, MultiBit 6.500s, EXP-edit 24.693s (Table 2)
Detection time: Ours 0.166s, KGW 0.067s, SWEET 0.127s, SIR 0.337s, MultiBit 0.610s, EXP-edit 155.045s (Table 2)
SWEET failure analysis: at (gamma,delta)=(0.25,3.0) only ~7 of 200 tokens are high-entropy and eligible for watermarking, so SWEET does not reach 100% TPR at 0% FPR
Learned parameters (Fig. 4): watermark logit delta ranges ~1.8-2.6 and splitting ratio gamma ~0.22-0.28 across POS categories; lower for ADJ/DET, higher for PUNCT
baselines_compared: MultiBit (Wang et al. 2023; message length 9 for FPR 0%, 7 for FPR 1%)
